did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Deep Learning, specifically an optimized MobileNetV2 architecture combined with a Coordinate Attention mechanism, to perform binary classification of skin lesions (benign vs. malignant) using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and optimization of a MobileNetV2 architecture with Coordinate Attention for skin cancer classification. It details specific methods (fine-tuning, autoencoder pre-training on CIFAR100), experiments on the HAM10000 dataset, and reports new quantitative results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) in Table II. It is published in the proceedings of the 25th Euromicro Conference on Digital System Design.

title: Towards Skin Cancer Self-Monitoring through an Optimized MobileNet with Coordinate Attention
journal: 25th Euromicro Conference on Digital System Design (DSD)
brief_summary: This study addresses the need for lightweight deep learning models for skin cancer self-monitoring by optimizing MobileNetV2 with a Coordinate Attention mechanism to classify benign versus malignant lesions. The authors utilized the HAM10000 dataset, balancing it via downsampling and applying minimal preprocessing including resizing and data augmentation. Fine-tuning was performed using an autoencoder pre-trained on the CIFAR100 dataset to mitigate overfitting. Experiments compared four scenarios involving regular and simplified MobileNetV2 architectures with and without the attention mechanism. The optimized simplified model (MbV2SCA) achieved a balanced sensitivity of 82.35% and specificity of 85.25%, with an overall accuracy of 83.93%. The results demonstrate that the proposed architecture significantly reduces model size and parameters while maintaining high performance, making it suitable for mobile deployment.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as the malignant class in the binary classification task. Positive samples were defined as those labeled as melanoma.

paper_terms: Actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma / Bowen’s disease
lesion_types_studied.details: Used as the benign class in the binary classification task. This group includes all other categories present in the HAM10000 dataset: Actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma / Bowen’s disease (akiec), basal cell carcinoma (bcc), benign keratosis-like lesions (solar lentigines / seborrheic keratoses and lichen-planus like keratoses, bkl), dermatofibroma (df), melanocytic nevi (nv), and vascular lesions (angiomas, angiokeratomas, pyogenic granulomas and hemorrhage, vasc).

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma (malignant) and non-melanoma (benign) classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between melanoma and non-melanoma skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant). Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and precision.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A well-known publicly available benchmark dataset for skin cancer, corresponding to the ISIC2018 dataset. It contains dermatoscopic images depicting various types of lesions including Actinic keratoses, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis-like lesions, dermatofibroma, melanoma, melanocytic nevi, and vascular lesions.

use.training: Used for training the MobileNetV2 models after balancing via downsampling and applying data augmentation (vertical and horizontal flips).
use.validation: Used for validation (10% of the balanced dataset).
use.testing: Used for testing (10% of the balanced dataset).

datasets_used.name: CIFAR100
datasets_used.description: A public image dataset used for pre-training an autoencoder to mitigate overfitting in the skin cancer classification models.

use.training: Used to pre-train the autoencoder which was then used to initialize/fine-tune the MobileNetV2 model.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision) and model complexity measures (size, parameters, training time) across four experimental scenarios.

HAM10000.split: Test set (10% of balanced dataset, 224 images)
HAM10000.experiment: Binary classification of Melanoma vs Non-melanoma
HAM10000.notes: MbV2SCA achieved the highest accuracy (83.93%) and best specificity (85.25%). It reduced model size by 54.8% compared to MbV2CA and parameters by 63.59% compared to MbV2CA. 196 out of 224 test images (87.50%) were classified in the same class across all configurations. Grad-CAM analysis showed the network focuses on the lesion region for positive predictions and lacks a clear pattern for negative ones.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning applied; convolutional layers pre-trained using an Autoencoder (AE) on the CIFAR100 dataset to mitigate overfitting. The AE was optimized using Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss.

architecture_details.ca_implementation: Applied in different inverted residual blocks. Generates positional information via average pooling kernels for horizontal and vertical coordinates, concatenated, then reduced via convolutional layer with reduction ratio r=32.

simplified_configuration.description: Simplified MobileNetV2 configuration (MbV2S/MbV2SCA) as per Table I

variants: MbV2SCA: Simplified MobileNetV2 with Coordinate Attention

classification_or_output_head.activation: Softmax (implied by CE loss definition with p(y_hat))

preprocessing_techniques: Dataset balancing via downsampling of the majority class to achieve equal number of images per class
Random splitting of the balanced dataset into training (80%), validation (10%), and test (10%) sets
Resizing images to input shape of 256x256x3
Data augmentation on the training set consisting of vertical and horizontal flips
Minimal preprocessing strategy to focus on model optimization rather than image enhancement

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the training set to augment the data. The paper notes that minimal preprocessing and data augmentation were used to focus on model optimization.

conclusions: The study successfully demonstrated that an optimized MobileNetV2 architecture combined with a Coordinate Attention (CA) mechanism can effectively classify skin lesions into benign and malignant classes, serving as a proof of concept for skin cancer self-monitoring.

The proposed simplified MobileNetV2 with Coordinate Attention (MbV2SCA) achieved a balanced sensitivity of 82.35% and specificity of 85.25%, with an overall accuracy of 83.93% on the HAM10000 dataset.

The MbV2SCA model significantly reduced model complexity, achieving a size of 67.4 MB and 970k parameters, which represents a reduction of more than half compared to the baseline regular MobileNetV2 configurations.

The addition of the Coordinate Attention mechanism helped compensate for the information loss caused by simplifying the network architecture, maintaining or improving accuracy while drastically reducing the number of parameters.
Fine-tuning the model using an autoencoder pre-trained on the CIFAR100 dataset was effective in mitigating overfitting and improving classification accuracy.

Grad-CAM analysis indicated that the network focuses on the lesion region for positive predictions (melanoma) but lacks a clear pattern for negative predictions (non-melanoma), suggesting that artifact removal (e.g., hairs) could potentially improve performance.

The lightweight nature of the optimized model makes it suitable for local deployment on mobile devices, addressing privacy and security concerns associated with cloud-based solutions for mHealth dermatological applications.
important_remarks: The study is a proof of concept for mobile deployment rather than a comprehensive clinical validation, limiting its immediate generalizability to real-world dermatological settings.

The HAM10000 dataset was artificially balanced via downsampling of the majority class, which may not reflect the true prevalence distribution of skin lesions in clinical practice and could bias model performance.

The model was trained and tested exclusively on dermatoscopic images; however, the authors note that mobile devices typically capture clinical (macroscopic) images, creating a domain gap between the training data and the intended deployment environment.

Grad-CAM analysis revealed that the network lacks a clear attention pattern for negative (benign) predictions, suggesting potential reliance on artifacts or background features rather than specific lesion characteristics for non-melanoma classification.

The paper did not perform external validation on independent datasets, relying solely on the HAM10000 dataset, which raises concerns about dataset bias and overfitting to specific image characteristics of this benchmark.

Minimal preprocessing was employed (only resizing and flips) to focus on architecture optimization, ignoring potential performance gains from artifact removal (e.g., hair, bubbles) which are common in dermatoscopic images.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported for the performance metrics, making it difficult to assess the statistical robustness of the improvements over baseline models.

The use of CIFAR100 for autoencoder pre-training introduces a domain mismatch, as CIFAR100 contains natural images rather than medical or dermatoscopic images, potentially limiting the effectiveness of the transfer learning strategy.
